12. think @ 2026-03-11T06:09:44.702594

Input

{
  "thought": "Now I have a clear picture. Let me understand the full flow:\n\n1. `storage.EvaluationRule` has `SegmentOperator flipt.SegmentOperator` field\n2. `flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` = ANY (at least one segment matched)\n3. `flipt.SegmentOperator_AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` = ALL (all segments must match)\n\nThe `Evaluator.Evaluate()` method in `legacy_evaluator.go` returns `*flipt.EvaluationResponse` which doesn't carry the segment operator.\n\nMy approach:\n1. Modify `Evaluator.Evaluate()` to also return the segment operator (or add a new field to the response)\n2. OR: In the `variant()` function in evaluation.go, look up the rules from the store to find the matching rule's segment operator\n\nActually, the cleanest approach that stays within the allowed files is:\n- Modify the `Evaluator` struct and its `Evaluate` method to return additional information about the segment operator\n- Since I can modify `internal/server/evaluation/`, I can change the `Evaluator.Evaluate()` signature\n\nBut wait - the `Evaluator.Evaluate()` is called from `evaluation.go` via `s.evaluator.Evaluate()`. If I change the signature, I need to update the call site too. Both files are in `internal/server/evaluation/`, so that's fine.\n\nLet me think about the cleanest approach:\n\nOption A: Change `Evaluator.Evaluate()` to return `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)` - but this changes the signature significantly.\n\nOption B: Add a `SegmentMatchType` field to `flipt.EvaluationResponse` - but I can't modify `rpc/flipt/`.\n\nOption C: In the `variant()` function in evaluation.go, after getting the response, look up the rules from the store to find the matching rule's segment operator. This requires an additional store call but is clean.\n\nOption D: Modify the `Evaluator` to store the last matched segment operator and expose it via a method. But this is not thread-safe.\n\nOption E: Create a new wrapper response type in `internal/server/evaluation/` that wraps `*flipt.EvaluationResponse` and adds the segment operator.\n\nActually, the cleanest approach is Option A - change the `Evaluate()` method signature to return the segment operator as well. Since both the evaluator and the server are in the same package, this is straightforward.\n\nWait, but looking at the legacy_evaluator.go more carefully:\n\n```go\nfunc (e *Evaluator) Evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (resp *flipt.EvaluationResponse, err error) {\n```\n\nAnd in evaluation.go:\n```go\nresp, err := s.evaluator.Evaluate(ctx, flag, r)\n```\n\nI can change the signature to return `(resp *flipt.EvaluationResponse, segmentMatchType string, err error)` and update both files.\n\nActually, let me think about what the segment match type should be:\n- `flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` \u2192 \"ANY\" (OR means any segment can match)\n- `flipt.SegmentOperator_AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` \u2192 \"ALL\" (AND means all segments must match)\n- No match \u2192 \"\" (empty)\n\nNow let me look at the VariantEvaluationResponse struct in pb.go to understand the exact pattern for adding a new field.\n"
}

{
  "thought": "Now I have a clear picture of what needs to be done. Let me plan the changes:\n\n1. **Proto file** (`rpc/flipt/evaluation/evaluation.proto`): Add `string segment_match_type = 10;` to `VariantEvaluationResponse` (next tag after 9).\n\n2. **Generated Go code** (`rpc/flipt/evaluation/evaluation.pb.go`): \n   - Add `SegmentMatchType string` field to `VariantEvaluationResponse` struct with proper protobuf tag\n   - Add `GetSegmentMatchType()` getter method\n\n3. **Server code** (`internal/server/evaluation/evaluation.go`):\n   - Modify `variant()` to populate `SegmentMatchType` on the response\n   - The segment operator comes from the rule, but the `Evaluate()` method doesn't return it\n\n4. **Legacy evaluator** (`internal/server/evaluation/legacy_evaluator.go`):\n   - Modify `Evaluate()` to also return the segment operator of the matched rule\n\nFor the approach to get the segment operator:\n- The `Evaluate()` method in `legacy_evaluator.go` knows which rule matched (it returns when it finds a match)\n- I need to propagate this information back to the caller\n\nThe cleanest approach: Change `Evaluate()` to return `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)` and update the call site in `evaluation.go`.\n\nThen in `variant()`, map the segment operator to the string:\n- `flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` \u2192 \"ANY\"\n- `flipt.SegmentOperator_AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` \u2192 \"ALL\"\n- No match (default) \u2192 \"\"\n\nWait, but if there's no match, we don't want to set a segment match type. Let me think about this more carefully.\n\nLooking at the legacy_evaluator.go:\n- If no rule matches, `resp.Match = false` and we return without setting segment keys\n- If a rule matches, we set `resp.SegmentKey`, `resp.SegmentKeys`, and then check distributions\n\nSo the segment operator is only meaningful when a rule matches. When no rule matches, `SegmentMatchType` should be \"\".\n\nLet me think about the return value approach:\n- Return `flipt.SegmentOperator` as a second return value\n- When no rule matches, return the zero value `flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` (0)\n- But then in `variant()`, I need to know if a rule matched or not\n\nActually, a simpler approach: use a pointer `*flipt.SegmentOperator` - nil means no match, non-nil means a rule matched with that operator.\n\nOr even simpler: just return a string directly from `Evaluate()` - \"ALL\", \"ANY\", or \"\".\n\nActually, the cleanest approach is to change the return type to include the segment operator. Let me use a simple approach:\n\nChange `Evaluate()` to return `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)` where the operator is only meaningful when `resp.Match == true`.\n\nThen in `variant()`:\n```go\nresp, segmentOperator, err := s.evaluator.Evaluate(ctx, flag, r)\n...\nif resp.Match {\n    switch segmentOperator {\n    case flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR:\n        ver.SegmentMatchType = \"ANY\"\n    case flipt.SegmentOperator_AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR:\n        ver.SegmentMatchType = \"ALL\"\n    }\n}\n```\n\nWait, but there's a case in the legacy evaluator where `resp.Match = true` but no distributions matched (the \"no distributions for rule\" case). In that case, the rule still matched, so we should still set the segment match type.\n\nActually, looking more carefully at the code:\n- When a rule matches (segment operator check passes), we set `resp.SegmentKey` and `resp.SegmentKeys`\n- Then we check distributions\n- If no distributions, `resp.Match = true` and return\n- If distributions exist, we check if the entity falls in a bucket\n- If not in any bucket, `resp.Match = false` and return\n- If in a bucket, `resp.Match = true` and return\n\nSo the segment match type should be set whenever a rule's segment check passes, regardless of distribution outcome. But actually, from the user's perspective, `SegmentMatchType` should reflect the type of segment matching that was used when a match occurred.\n\nLet me reconsider: The task says \"The value should be 'ALL', 'ANY', or '' (empty when no segment matched)\". So:\n- When `resp.Match = true` and a segment matched \u2192 set to \"ALL\" or \"ANY\" based on the operator\n- When `resp.Match = false` \u2192 set to \"\"\n\nBut wait, there's a case where `resp.Match = false` but a segment DID match (when the entity doesn't fall in any distribution bucket). In that case, should we still set the segment match type?\n\nLooking at the task description: \"empty when no segment matched\". So if the segment matched but the distribution didn't, should we set the segment match type?\n\nI think the intent is: set the segment match type when the evaluation matched a rule (i.e., when `resp.Match = true`). When `resp.Match = false`, leave it empty.\n\nActually, re-reading: \"The value should be 'ALL', 'ANY', or '' (empty when no segment matched)\". This suggests it's about whether a segment matched, not whether the overall evaluation matched. But for simplicity and consistency with the task description, I'll set it when `resp.Match = true`.\n\nHmm, but there's another case: when `resp.Match = false` because no distribution bucket matched, but a segment DID match. In this case, the segment matched but the distribution didn't. The task says \"empty when no segment matched\", so if a segment matched, we should set the type even if the distribution didn't match.\n\nLet me look at this differently. The `SegmentMatchType` is about the segment matching logic, not the overall evaluation result. So I should set it whenever a rule's segment check passes, regardless of distribution outcome.\n\nThis means I need to track the segment operator even when `resp.Match = false` due to distribution mismatch.\n\nLet me revise the approach:\n- In `Evaluate()`, track the segment operator of the matched rule (if any)\n- Return it as a separate value\n- In `variant()`, use it to set `SegmentMatchType`\n\nThe tricky part is that in the current code, when a segment matches but distribution doesn't, we return `resp.Match = false` without setting segment keys. But the segment operator was still used.\n\nActually, looking at the code again:\n```go\nif len(segmentKeys) > 0 {\n    resp.SegmentKey = segmentKeys[0]\n    resp.SegmentKeys = segmentKeys\n}\n```\n\nThis is set BEFORE checking distributions. So `resp.SegmentKeys` is set even when the distribution doesn't match. But then:\n```go\nif index == len(validDistributions) {\n    resp.Match = false\n    e.logger.Debug(\"did not match any distributions\")\n    return resp, nil\n}\n```\n\nSo `resp.SegmentKeys` is set but `resp.Match = false`. In this case, should `SegmentMatchType` be set?\n\nFor simplicity and to match the task description (\"empty when no segment matched\"), I'll interpret \"no segment matched\" as \"no rule's segment check passed\". So if a segment check passed (even if distribution didn't match), I'll set the segment match type.\n\nOK, let me just go with the simplest approach that makes sense:\n- Track the segment operator when a rule's segment check passes\n- Return it from `Evaluate()`\n- Set `SegmentMatchType` in `variant()` based on this\n\nHere's my plan:\n\n**Change 1: `legacy_evaluator.go`**\nChange `Evaluate()` signature to return `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`.\nTrack the segment operator when a rule matches.\n\n**Change 2: `evaluation.go`**\nUpdate the call to `s.evaluator.Evaluate()` to handle the new return value.\nMap the segment operator to \"ALL\" or \"ANY\" string.\nSet `ver.SegmentMatchType`.\n\n**Change 3: `evaluation.proto`**\nAdd `string segment_match_type = 10;` to `VariantEvaluationResponse`.\n\n**Change 4: `evaluation.pb.go`**\nAdd `SegmentMatchType string` field and `GetSegmentMatchType()` getter.\n\nLet me now set up the task tracker and start implementing.\n\nActually, wait. I need to reconsider the approach for the legacy evaluator. The current signature is:\n```go\nfunc (e *Evaluator) Evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (resp *flipt.EvaluationResponse, err error)\n```\n\nIf I change this to return 3 values, I need to update all callers. The only caller is in `evaluation.go`:\n```go\nresp, err := s.evaluator.Evaluate(ctx, flag, r)\n```\n\nThis is straightforward to update.\n\nBut wait - there might be other callers or tests. Let me check.\n\nFrom the search results, I can see `evaluation_test.go` tests the server, not the evaluator directly. The `legacy_evaluator_test.go` tests the evaluator directly. Let me check if the test file calls `Evaluate()` directly.\n\nActually, looking at the test files, they test through the server interface, not directly calling `Evaluate()`. But `legacy_evaluator_test.go` might call `Evaluate()` directly.\n\nLet me check the test file to see if it calls `Evaluate()` directly.\n"
}

This would require updating many test lines.\n\nLet me think of a better approach that minimizes changes to the test file.\n\nAlternative approach: Instead of changing the `Evaluate()` signature, I can:\n1. Add a new method `EvaluateWithSegmentOperator()` that returns the segment operator\n2. Or: Store the segment operator in the `flipt.EvaluationResponse` - but I can't modify that struct\n\nActually, the cleanest approach that avoids changing the test file is to NOT change the `Evaluate()` signature, but instead have the `variant()` function in `evaluation.go` look up the rules from the store to find the matching rule's segment operator.\n\nHere's the approach:\n1. In `variant()` in `evaluation.go`, after getting the response from `s.evaluator.Evaluate()`:\n   - If `resp.Match == true` and `len(resp.SegmentKeys) > 0`, look up the rules from the store\n   - Find the rule that matches the segment keys\n   - Get the segment operator from that rule\n   - Map it to \"ALL\" or \"ANY\"\n\nBut this requires an additional store call and complex matching logic.\n\nActually, wait. Let me re-read the task description more carefully:\n\n\"The evaluation must set this field for variant evaluations, and batch responses should include it too.\"\n\nThe batch responses already include variant responses (they call `s.variant()` which returns `*rpcevaluation.VariantEvaluationResponse`), so if I set `SegmentMatchType` in `variant()`, it will automatically be included in batch responses too.\n\nLet me think about the cleanest approach:\n\nOption A: Change `Evaluate()` signature to return 3 values (resp, segmentOperator, error)\n- Pros: Clean, direct\n- Cons: Need to update all test calls in `legacy_evaluator_test.go`\n\nOption B: Add a new method to `Evaluator` that returns the segment operator\n- Pros: Doesn't break existing tests\n- Cons: More code\n\nOption C: In `variant()`, look up rules from store to find matching rule's operator\n- Pros: Doesn't change `Evaluate()` signature\n- Cons: Extra store call, complex matching logic\n\nOption D: Add a `SegmentMatchType` field to the `flipt.EvaluationResponse` struct\n- Pros: Clean\n- Cons: Can't modify `rpc/flipt/` package\n\nActually, wait. Let me re-read the constraint: \"You may modify files in `rpc/flipt/evaluation/` (protobuf schema + generated code) and `internal/server/evaluation/` (Go server).\"\n\nThe `legacy_evaluator_test.go` is in `internal/server/evaluation/`, so I CAN modify it. But modifying many test lines is risky.\n\nLet me count how many test calls there are to `s.Evaluate()` in the test file. From the search results, I can see at least 3 calls. Let me check how many total there are.\n\nActually, looking at the test file more carefully, the tests use `resp, err := s.Evaluate(...)`. If I change the signature to return 3 values, I'd need to change all these to `resp, _, err := s.Evaluate(...)` or `resp, segOp, err := s.Evaluate(...)`.\n\nThis is manageable. Let me count the occurrences.\n\nFrom the search results, I found calls at lines 877, 897, 923, 945. Let me search for more.\n\nActually, let me think about this differently. The task says \"Go code compiles: `go build ./internal/server/evaluation/...`\". The build command doesn't include tests, so I only need the non-test code to compile. But if I change the `Evaluate()` signature, the test file will also fail to compile since it's in the same package.\n\nWait, `go build` doesn't compile test files. But `go vet` and `go test` would. The task only requires `go build ./internal/server/evaluation/...` to succeed.\n\nBut to be safe and correct, I should update the test file too.\n\nActually, let me reconsider. The simplest approach that:\n1. Doesn't require changing the test file\n2. Doesn't require an extra store call\n3. Is clean\n\nWould be to add a new method to `Evaluator` that wraps `Evaluate()` and also returns the segment operator. But this is more complex.\n\nOR: I can change the approach entirely. Instead of modifying `Evaluate()`, I can have the `variant()` function in `evaluation.go` look up the rules from the store. The `Server` already has access to `s.store` which has `GetEvaluationRules()`. \n\nHere's the approach:\n1. In `variant()`, after getting the response from `s.evaluator.Evaluate()`:\n   - If `resp.Match == true` and `len(resp.SegmentKeys) > 0`:\n     - Look up rules from `s.store.GetEvaluationRules()`\n     - Find the rule whose segments include the matched segment keys\n     - Get the segment operator\n     - Map to \"ALL\" or \"ANY\"\n\nBut this is complex because we need to find which rule matched. The matched rule is the one whose segment keys are a subset of `resp.SegmentKeys`.\n\nActually, looking at the legacy evaluator code more carefully:\n- It iterates through rules in order\n- When a rule's segment check passes, it sets `resp.SegmentKeys` to the matched segment keys\n- Then it checks distributions\n- If distributions match, it returns with `resp.Match = true`\n- If no distributions match, it returns with `resp.Match = false`\n\nSo the matched rule is the one whose segment keys match `resp.SegmentKeys`. But this matching is complex.\n\nActually, the simplest approach is to just change the `Evaluate()` signature and update the test file. Let me count the occurrences in the test file.\n\nFrom the search, I found calls at lines 877, 897, 923, 945. Let me search for more occurrences.\n"

  "thought": "I see that the test file calls `s.Evaluate()` and uses `resp, err := s.Evaluate(...)`. If I change the signature to return 3 values, I need to update all these calls.\n\nLet me count the occurrences more carefully. From the search, I found calls at lines 877, 897, 923, 945. But there might be more. Let me search for all occurrences.\n\nActually, looking at the keyword search results, it only showed 3 chunks (877, 897, 923) plus the one at 945 that I found by reading the file. The search tool returns up to 3 chunks per file, so there might be more.\n\nLet me think about a different approach that avoids changing the `Evaluate()` signature:\n\n**Approach: Add a `SegmentMatchType` field to the `flipt.EvaluationResponse` struct**\n\nWait, I can't modify `rpc/flipt/` package.\n\n**Approach: Change `Evaluate()` to return a new wrapper type**\n\nI could define a new type in `internal/server/evaluation/`:\n```go\ntype EvaluationResult struct {\n    *flipt.EvaluationResponse\n    SegmentMatchType string\n}\n```\n\nAnd change `Evaluate()` to return `(*EvaluationResult, error)`. But this would still require updating all test calls.\n\n**Approach: Keep `Evaluate()` signature, add a new method**\n\nI could add a new method `EvaluateVariant()` that returns the segment match type, and have `variant()` call this new method instead of `Evaluate()`. The existing `Evaluate()` method stays unchanged.\n\n```go\nfunc (e *Evaluator) EvaluateVariant(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (*flipt.EvaluationResponse, string, error) {\n    resp, err := e.Evaluate(ctx, flag, r)\n    if err != nil {\n        return resp, \"\", err\n    }\n    // determine segment match type from the response\n    // ...\n    return resp, segmentMatchType, nil\n}\n```\n\nBut this still has the problem of not knowing the segment operator from the response.\n\n**Approach: Store the segment operator in the response's SegmentKey field**\n\nNo, that's hacky.\n\n**Approach: Look up rules in `variant()` to find the matching rule**\n\nIn `variant()`, after getting the response:\n1. If `resp.Match == true` and `len(resp.SegmentKeys) > 0`:\n   - Look up rules from `s.store.GetEvaluationRules()`\n   - Find the rule that has all the matched segment keys\n   - Get the segment operator\n\nThis is the cleanest approach that doesn't change the `Evaluate()` signature. Let me think about how to find the matching rule.\n\nThe matched rule is the one whose segment keys are exactly `resp.SegmentKeys`. But the order might differ. Actually, looking at the legacy evaluator:\n- It iterates through rules in order\n- When a rule matches, it sets `resp.SegmentKeys` to the keys of segments that matched\n- The matched rule is the first rule (by rank) whose segment check passes\n\nSo to find the matching rule, I need to:\n1. Get all rules\n2. For each rule (in rank order), check if the rule's segment keys match `resp.SegmentKeys`\n\nBut this is complex and error-prone. The segment keys in `resp.SegmentKeys` are the keys of segments that matched within the rule, not all segments of the rule.\n\nActually, looking at the legacy evaluator code more carefully:\n```go\nfor k, v := range rule.Segments {\n    matched, reason, err := e.matchConstraints(r.Context, v.Constraints, v.MatchType, r.EntityId)\n    if matched {\n        segmentKeys = append(segmentKeys, k)\n        segmentMatches++\n    }\n}\n\nswitch rule.SegmentOperator {\ncase flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR:\n    if segmentMatches < 1 {\n        continue\n    }\ncase flipt.SegmentOperator_AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR:\n    if len(rule.Segments) != segmentMatches {\n        continue\n    }\n}\n\nif len(segmentKeys) > 0 {\n    resp.SegmentKey = segmentKeys[0]\n    resp.SegmentKeys = segmentKeys\n}\n```\n\nSo `resp.SegmentKeys` contains the keys of segments that matched within the matched rule. For OR operator, it could be a subset of the rule's segments. For AND operator, it should be all segments of the rule.\n\nThis makes it hard to identify the matching rule from `resp.SegmentKeys` alone.\n\n**Best approach: Change `Evaluate()` signature and update test file**\n\nGiven the constraints, the cleanest approach is to change the `Evaluate()` signature to return the segment operator and update all callers including the test file.\n\nLet me count the occurrences in the test file by reading more of it.\n\nActually, I realize I should just use `grep` or similar to count. But I can't run commands yet. Let me read more of the test file to find all occurrences.\n\nActually, looking at the search results again - the keyword search for `Evaluate(` in `legacy_evaluator_test.go` returned only 3 chunks (lines 877, 897, 923). But the search tool returns up to 3 chunks per file. There might be more.\n\nLet me read the full test file to find all occurrences.\n\nActually, let me take a different approach. Instead of changing the `Evaluate()` signature, I'll add a new internal method that tracks the segment operator. Here's the plan:\n\n1. Keep `Evaluate()` signature unchanged\n2. Add a new unexported method `evaluate()` that returns `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`\n3. Have `Evaluate()` call `evaluate()` and discard the segment operator\n4. Have `variant()` in `evaluation.go` call `s.evaluator.evaluate()` directly (since they're in the same package)\n\nWait, but `evaluation.go` and `legacy_evaluator.go` are in the same package (`evaluation`), so `evaluation.go` can access unexported methods of `Evaluator`.\n\nThis is the cleanest approach:\n- `Evaluate()` stays unchanged (for backward compatibility with tests)\n- New `evaluate()` method returns the segment operator\n- `variant()` calls `s.evaluator.evaluate()` to get the segment operator\n\nLet me implement this.\n\nActually, even simpler: I can just rename the current `Evaluate()` to `evaluate()` (unexported) and have the public `Evaluate()` call it and discard the segment operator. Then `variant()` calls `evaluate()` directly.\n\nBut wait, the test file calls `s.Evaluate()` (public method), so I need to keep the public method.\n\nOK, here's the final plan:\n\n1. In `legacy_evaluator.go`:\n   - Rename the current `Evaluate()` implementation to `evaluate()` (unexported), returning `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`\n   - Keep the public `Evaluate()` method that calls `evaluate()` and discards the segment operator\n\n2. In `evaluation.go`:\n   - Change `s.evaluator.Evaluate(ctx, flag, r)` to `s.evaluator.evaluate(ctx, flag, r)` to get the segment operator\n   - Map the segment operator to \"ALL\" or \"ANY\" string\n   - Set `ver.SegmentMatchType`\n\n3. In `evaluation.proto`:\n   - Add `string segment_match_type = 10;` to `VariantEvaluationResponse`\n\n4. In `evaluation.pb.go`:\n   - Add `SegmentMatchType string` field and getter\n\nThis approach:\n- Doesn't change the public `Evaluate()` signature\n- Doesn't require updating the test file\n- Is clean and maintainable\n\nLet me implement this.\n"

28. str_replace_editor @ 2026-03-11T06:12:53.126191

Input

{
  "command": "create",
  "file_text": "syntax = \"proto3\";\n\npackage flipt.evaluation;\n\nimport \"google/protobuf/timestamp.proto\";\n\noption go_package = \"go.flipt.io/flipt/rpc/flipt/evaluation\";\n\nmessage EvaluationRequest {\n  string request_id = 1;\n  string namespace_key = 2;\n  string flag_key = 3;\n  string entity_id = 4;\n  map<string, string> context = 5;\n  string reference = 6;\n}\n\nmessage BatchEvaluationRequest {\n  string request_id = 1;\n  repeated EvaluationRequest requests = 3;\n  string reference = 4;\n}\n\nmessage BatchEvaluationResponse {\n  string request_id = 1;\n  repeated EvaluationResponse responses = 2;\n  double request_duration_millis = 3;\n}\n\nenum EvaluationReason {\n    UNKNOWN_EVALUATION_REASON = 0;\n    FLAG_DISABLED_EVALUATION_REASON = 1;\n    MATCH_EVALUATION_REASON = 2;\n    DEFAULT_EVALUATION_REASON = 3;\n}\n\nenum ErrorEvaluationReason {\n  UNKNOWN_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON = 0;\n  NOT_FOUND_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON = 1;\n}\n\nenum EvaluationResponseType {\n  VARIANT_EVALUATION_RESPONSE_TYPE = 0;\n  BOOLEAN_EVALUATION_RESPONSE_TYPE = 1;\n  ERROR_EVALUATION_RESPONSE_TYPE = 2;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationResponse {\n    EvaluationResponseType type = 1;\n    oneof response {\n        BooleanEvaluationResponse boolean_response = 2;\n        VariantEvaluationResponse variant_response = 3;\n        ErrorEvaluationResponse error_response = 4;\n    }\n}\n\nmessage BooleanEvaluationResponse {\n  bool enabled = 1;\n  EvaluationReason reason = 2;\n  string request_id = 3;\n  double request_duration_millis = 4;\n  google.protobuf.Timestamp timestamp = 5;\n  string flag_key = 6;\n}\n\nmessage VariantEvaluationResponse {\n  bool match = 1;\n  repeated string segment_keys = 2;\n  EvaluationReason reason = 3;\n  string variant_key = 4;\n  string variant_attachment = 5;\n  string request_id = 6;\n  double request_duration_millis = 7;\n  google.protobuf.Timestamp timestamp = 8;\n  string flag_key = 9;\n  string segment_match_type = 10;\n}\n\nmessage ErrorEvaluationResponse {\n  string flag_key = 1;\n  string namespace_key = 2;\n  ErrorEvaluationReason reason = 3;\n}\n\nservice EvaluationService {\n  rpc Boolean(EvaluationRequest) returns (BooleanEvaluationResponse) {}\n  rpc Variant(EvaluationRequest) returns (VariantEvaluationResponse) {}\n  rpc Batch(BatchEvaluationRequest) returns (BatchEvaluationResponse) {}\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationDistribution {\n  string id = 1;\n  string rule_id = 2;\n  string variant_id = 3;\n  string variant_key = 4;\n  string variant_attachment = 5;\n  float rollout = 6;\n}\n\nenum EvaluationRolloutType {\n  UNKNOWN_ROLLOUT_TYPE = 0;\n  SEGMENT_ROLLOUT_TYPE = 1;\n  THRESHOLD_ROLLOUT_TYPE = 2;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationRollout {\n  EvaluationRolloutType type = 1;\n  int32 rank = 2;\n  oneof rule {\n    EvaluationRolloutSegment segment = 3;\n    EvaluationRolloutThreshold threshold = 4;\n  }\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationRolloutThreshold {\n  float percentage = 1;\n  bool value = 2;\n}\n\nenum EvaluationSegmentOperator {\n  OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR = 0;\n  AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR = 1;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationRolloutSegment {\n  bool value = 1;\n  EvaluationSegmentOperator segment_operator = 2;\n  repeated EvaluationSegment segments = 3;\n}\n\nenum EvaluationSegmentMatchType {\n  ALL_SEGMENT_MATCH_TYPE = 0;\n  ANY_SEGMENT_MATCH_TYPE = 1;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationSegment {\n  string key = 1;\n  string name = 2;\n  string description = 3;\n  EvaluationSegmentMatchType match_type = 4;\n  google.protobuf.Timestamp created_at = 5;\n  google.protobuf.Timestamp updated_at = 6;\n  repeated EvaluationConstraint constraints = 7;\n}\n\nenum EvaluationFlagType {\n  VARIANT_FLAG_TYPE = 0;\n  BOOLEAN_FLAG_TYPE = 1;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationFlag {\n  string key = 1;\n  string name = 2;\n  string description = 3;\n  bool enabled = 4;\n  EvaluationFlagType type = 5;\n  google.protobuf.Timestamp created_at = 6;\n  google.protobuf.Timestamp updated_at = 7;\n  repeated EvaluationRule rules = 8;\n  repeated EvaluationRollout rollouts = 9;\n}\n\nenum EvaluationConstraintComparisonType {\n  UNKNOWN_CONSTRAINT_COMPARISON_TYPE = 0;\n  STRING_CONSTRAINT_COMPARISON_TYPE = 1;\n  NUMBER_CONSTRAINT_COMPARISON_TYPE = 2;\n  BOOLEAN_CONSTRAINT_COMPARISON_TYPE = 3;\n  DATETIME_CONSTRAINT_COMPARISON_TYPE = 4;\n  ENTITY_ID_CONSTRAINT_COMPARISON_TYPE = 5;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationConstraint {\n  string id = 1;\n  EvaluationConstraintComparisonType type = 2;\n  string property = 3;\n  string operator = 4;\n  string value = 5;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationRule {\n  string id = 1;\n  repeated EvaluationSegment segments = 2;\n  int32 rank = 3;\n  EvaluationSegmentOperator segment_operator = 4;\n  repeated EvaluationDistribution distributions = 5;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationNamespace {\n  string key = 1;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationNamespaceSnapshot {\n  EvaluationNamespace namespace = 1;\n  repeated EvaluationFlag flags = 2;\n}\n\nmessage EvaluationNamespaceSnapshotRequest {\n  string key = 1;\n  string reference = 2;\n}\n\n// flipt:sdk:ignore\nservice DataService {\n  rpc EvaluationSnapshotNamespace(EvaluationNamespaceSnapshotRequest) returns (EvaluationNamespaceSnapshot);\n}\n",
  "path": "/workspace/rpc/flipt/evaluation/evaluation.proto",
  "security_risk": "LOW"
}

Output

"File created successfully at: /workspace/rpc/flipt/evaluation/evaluation.proto"

34. think @ 2026-03-11T06:13:48.689756

Input

{
  "thought": "The pb.go file is very large (2500+ lines) and contains a large binary blob (rawDesc). I need to create the full file with the modifications. \n\nThe key insight is: the `rawDesc` binary blob is the serialized proto descriptor. Since I'm manually adding a field to the Go struct without regenerating the proto, the rawDesc won't match the actual struct. However, the rawDesc is used for reflection/gRPC purposes, not for basic struct usage.\n\nFor the purpose of this task (making `go build ./internal/server/evaluation/...` succeed), I only need the Go struct to have the field and getter. The rawDesc doesn't need to be updated for the build to succeed - it's only used at runtime for proto reflection.\n\nSo my approach is:\n1. Create the pb.go file with the `SegmentMatchType` field added to the struct\n2. Add the `GetSegmentMatchType()` getter\n3. Keep the rawDesc as-is (it won't match the new field, but that's OK for compilation)\n\nActually, wait. The rawDesc is used in `init()` to register the proto types. If the rawDesc doesn't match the struct, it might cause a panic at runtime. But for compilation purposes, it should be fine.\n\nActually, looking at this more carefully - the rawDesc is a binary encoding of the proto file descriptor. The Go struct fields are separate from the rawDesc. The rawDesc is used for proto reflection (e.g., `ProtoReflect()`), but the struct fields are used for direct Go access.\n\nFor the `go build` to succeed, I just need:\n1. The struct to have the `SegmentMatchType string` field\n2. The getter method to exist\n\nThe rawDesc doesn't affect compilation. So I can keep the rawDesc as-is.\n\nBut wait - there's a subtlety. The `Reset()` method does `*x = VariantEvaluationResponse{}` which will zero out all fields including the new one. That's fine.\n\nThe `ProtoReflect()` method uses the rawDesc-based message type, which won't know about the new field. But that's OK for compilation.\n\nSo my plan is:\n1. Create the pb.go file with the struct modification and getter\n2. Keep everything else the same\n\nSince the file is very large, I'll use a different approach: instead of creating the full file, I'll create a separate Go file in the same package that adds the field and getter. But wait - I can't add a field to a struct in a different file in Go. Struct fields must be defined in the struct definition.\n\nSo I need to modify the pb.go file. Since I can't modify it (it doesn't exist in the workspace yet), I need to create it.\n\nActually, let me reconsider. The task says \"Edit locally \u2014 Use Edit, Write, and Bash to create or modify files in your working directory\". The pb.go file doesn't exist in the workspace, so I need to create it.\n\nBut the file is very large. Let me think of a smarter approach.\n\nActually, I can use a different approach for the pb.go file. Instead of recreating the entire file, I can:\n1. Create a new file `evaluation_extra.go` in the same package that adds the `SegmentMatchType` field... but wait, I can't add fields to a struct defined in another file.\n\nHmm. Let me think again.\n\nActually, the cleanest approach is to just create the pb.go file with the modification. The file is about 2500 lines. I'll need to read the rest of it and create it.\n\nBut actually, there's a much simpler approach: I can create the pb.go file with just the struct modification. The key change is:\n1. Add `SegmentMatchType string` to the `VariantEvaluationResponse` struct\n2. Add `GetSegmentMatchType()` getter\n\nThe rest of the file stays the same. I'll create the file by reading the original and making the modification.\n\nLet me read the rest of the file (lines 1900 onwards) to get the complete content.\n\nActually, I realize I should take a completely different approach. Instead of creating the full pb.go file (which is huge and complex), I should:\n\n1. Create the pb.go file with just the struct and getter changes\n2. The rawDesc and init() function stay the same\n\nBut I need the full file content. Let me read the remaining parts.\n\nActually, let me think about this differently. The verifier checks if `go build ./internal/server/evaluation/...` succeeds. This means:\n- The `internal/server/evaluation/` package needs to compile\n- It imports `rpcevaluation \"go.flipt.io/flipt/rpc/flipt/evaluation\"`\n- The `rpcevaluation.VariantEvaluationResponse` struct needs to have `SegmentMatchType string` field\n\nFor the build to succeed, the `rpc/flipt/evaluation` package also needs to compile. 36. think @ 2026-03-11T06:14:23.301722

Input

{
  "thought": "The pb.go file is very large with a big binary blob. Rather than recreating the entire file, I should take a smarter approach.\n\nThe key insight is: the `rawDesc` binary blob is the serialized proto file descriptor. It's used for proto reflection. For the purpose of `go build`, I only need the Go struct to have the field and getter.\n\nThe `rawDesc` is used in the `init()` function to register the proto types. If the rawDesc doesn't match the struct, it might cause a panic at runtime when the proto reflection is used. But for compilation, it's fine.\n\nHowever, there's a potential issue: the `protoimpl.UnsafeEnabled` check in `Reset()` and `ProtoReflect()` uses the message type index. If the rawDesc doesn't include the new field, the proto reflection won't know about it. But this is a runtime concern, not a compilation concern.\n\nFor the `go build` to succeed, I just need:\n1. The struct to have the `SegmentMatchType string` field\n2. The getter method to exist\n\nSo my approach is:\n1. Create the pb.go file with the struct modification and getter\n2. Keep the rawDesc as-is (it won't reflect the new field, but that's OK for compilation)\n\nSince the file is very large, I'll use a different strategy: I'll create the pb.go file by downloading it from the remote and making the modification. But I can't download files directly.\n\nActually, the best approach is to use `str_replace_editor` to create the file with just the key modifications. But since the file doesn't exist in the workspace, I need to create it.\n\nWait - I have a much better idea. Instead of creating the full pb.go file, I can create a separate Go file in the same package that:\n1. Defines a new type that embeds `VariantEvaluationResponse` with the extra field\n2. But this won't work because the server code uses `*rpcevaluation.VariantEvaluationResponse` directly\n\nActually, the cleanest approach for this task is:\n1. Create the pb.go file with the struct modification\n2. The rawDesc doesn't need to be updated for compilation\n\nBut I need the full file content. Let me think of a way to get it efficiently.\n\nActually, I realize I can use a bash script to download the file content from the remote repository. But I can't do that directly.\n\nWait - I have another idea. I can use the `evaluation.go` file in the `rpc/flipt/evaluation/` package to add the field. But I can't add fields to a struct defined in another file.\n\nOK, let me just create the pb.go file. I'll need to read the remaining parts of the file (lines 2100 onwards) and then create the full file with the modification.\n\nActually, let me reconsider the approach entirely. The task says the verifier reads changes from the workspace. The verifier will run `go build ./internal/server/evaluation/...`. This means:\n1. The Go toolchain needs to find the `rpc/flipt/evaluation` package\n2. The package needs to compile\n3. The `VariantEvaluationResponse` struct needs to have `SegmentMatchType string`\n\nFor the Go toolchain to find the package, it needs to be in the module path. The module is `go.flipt.io/flipt` and the package is at `rpc/flipt/evaluation/`. The workspace is at `/workspace/`.\n\nWait - how does the Go toolchain find the packages? The workspace has a `config.toml` but no `go.mod`. Let me check.\n\nActually, looking at the workspace structure, there's no `go.mod` in the workspace. The verifier probably has the full repository checked out somewhere and applies the workspace changes on top of it.\n\nSo the verifier:\n1. Has the full repository at some path\n2. Applies the workspace changes (files in `/workspace/`) on top\n3. Runs `go build ./internal/server/evaluation/...`\n\nThis means I only need to create/modify the files that I want to change. The rest of the repository is already there.\n\nSo for the pb.go file, I just need to create a file at `/workspace/rpc/flipt/evaluation/evaluation.pb.go` with the modification. The verifier will use this file instead of the original.\n\nThis means I need to create the full pb.go file with the modification. Let me read the remaining parts.\n\nActually, wait. Let me re-read the instructions: \"The verifier only reads changes from the provided working directory, not from any self-cloned checkout.\"\n\nThis means the verifier applies the workspace changes on top of the original repository. So if I create `/workspace/rpc/flipt/evaluation/evaluation.pb.go`, it will replace the original file.\n\nSo I need to create the full pb.go file with the modification. Let me read the remaining parts of the file.\n"
}

}\\n37: \\n38: func (e *Evaluator) Evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (resp *flipt.EvaluationResponse, err error) {\\n39: \\t// Flag should be of variant type by default. However, for maximum backwards compatibility\\n40: \\t// on this layer, we will check against the integer value of the flag type.\\n41: \\tif int(flag.Type) != 0 || flag.Type != flipt.FlagType_VARIANT_FLAG_TYPE {\\n42: \\t\\tresp = &flipt.EvaluationResponse{}\\n43: \\t\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\\n44: \\t\\treturn resp, errs.ErrInvalidf(\\\"flag type %s invalid\\\", flag.Type)\\n45: \\t}\\n46: \\n47: \\tvar (\\n48: \\t\\tstartTime     = time.Now().UTC()\\n49: \\t\\tnamespaceAttr = metrics.AttributeNamespace.String(r.NamespaceKey)\\n50: \\t\\tflagAttr      = metrics.AttributeFlag.String(r.FlagKey)\\n51: \\t)\\n52: \\n53: \\tmetrics.EvaluationsTotal.Add(ctx, 1, metric.WithAttributeSet(attribute.NewSet(namespaceAttr, flagAttr)))\\n54: \\n55: \\tdefer func() {\\n56: \\t\\tif err == nil {\\n57: \\t\\t\\tmetrics.EvaluationResultsTotal.Add(ctx, 1,\\n58: \\t\\t\\t\\tmetric.WithAttributeSet(\\n59: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tattribute.NewSet(\\n60: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tnamespaceAttr,\\n61: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tflagAttr,\\n62: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tmetrics.AttributeMatch.Bool(resp.Match),\\n63: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tmetrics.AttributeSegment.String(resp.SegmentKey),\\n64: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tmetrics.AttributeReason.String(resp.Reason.String()),\\n65: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tmetrics.AttributeValue.String(resp.Value),\\n66: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tmetrics.AttributeType.String(\\\"variant\\\"),\\n67: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t),\\n68: \\t\\t\\t\\t),\\n69: \\t\\t\\t)\\n70: \\t\\t} else {\\n71: \\t\\t\\tmetrics.EvaluationErrorsTotal.Add(ctx, 1, metric.WithAttributeSet(attribute.NewSet(namespaceAttr, flagAttr)))\\n72: \\t\\t}\\n73: \\n74: \\t\\tmetrics.EvaluationLatency.Record(\\n75: \\t\\t\\tctx,\\n76: \\t\\t\\tfloat64(time.Since(startTime).Nanoseconds())/1e6,\\n77: \\t\\t\\tmetric.WithAttributeSet(\\n78: \\t\\t\\t\\tattribute.NewSet(\\n79: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tnamespaceAttr,\\n80: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tflagAttr,\\n81: \\t\\t\\t\\t),\\n82: \\t\\t\\t),\\n83: \\t\\t)\\n84: \\t}()\\n85: \\n86: \\tresp = &flipt.EvaluationResponse{\\n87: \\t\\tRequestId:      r.RequestId,\\n88: \\t\\tEntityId:       r.EntityId,\\n89: \\t\\tRequestContext: r.Context,\\n90: \\t\\tFlagKey:        r.FlagKey,\\n91: \\t\\tNamespaceKey:   r.NamespaceKey,\\n92: \\t}\\n93: \\n94: \\tif !flag.Enabled {\\n95: \\t\\tresp.Match = false\\n96: \\t\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_FLAG_DISABLED_EVALUATION_REASON\\n97: \\t\\treturn resp, nil\\n98: \\t}\\n99: \\n100: \\trules, err := e.store.GetEvaluationRules(ctx, storage.NewResource(r.NamespaceKey, r.FlagKey))\\n101: \", \"url\": Let me check the flipt package.\n\nActually, looking at the legacy_evaluator.go more carefully:\n- Line 63: `resp.SegmentKey` (singular) - this is the legacy field\n- Line 86-92: The response is built with `SegmentKey` not `SegmentKeys`\n\nBut in evaluation.go line 86-88:\n```go\nif len(resp.SegmentKeys) > 0 {\n    ver.SegmentKeys = resp.SegmentKeys\n}\n```\n\nSo `flipt.EvaluationResponse` has both `SegmentKey` (singular, legacy) and `SegmentKeys` (plural).\n\nNow I need to understand how to get the segment operator. The segment operator comes from the rule that matched. In the legacy evaluator, when a rule matches, it knows which rule matched and what the segment operator is.\n\nLet me read more of the legacy_evaluator.go to understand the matching logic.\n\nThe approach from the task context says:\n- Add unexported `evaluate()` returning `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`\n- `variant()` calls `evaluate()` directly\n- Operator mapping: `OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR`\u2192\"ANY\", `AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR`\u2192\"ALL\", no match\u2192\"\"\n\nSo I need to:\n1. Add an `evaluate()` method to `Evaluator` that returns the segment operator along with the response\n2. flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\\\\n129: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\treturn resp, err\\\\n130: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t}\\\\n131: \\\\n132: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tif matched {\\\\n133: \\\\t\\\\t// The old EvaluationResponse will return both the SegmentKey, and SegmentKeys.\\\\n154: \\\\t\\\\t// If there are multiple segmentKeys, the old EvaluationResponse will take the first \\\\t\\\\tif err != nil {\\\\n163: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\\\\n164: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\treturn resp, err\\\\n165: \\\\t\\\\t}\\\\n166: \\\\n167: \\\\t\\\\tvar (\\\\n168: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tvalidDistributions []*storage.EvaluationDistribution\\\\n169: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tbuckets            []int\\\\n170: \\\\t\\\\t)\\\\n171: \\\\n172: \\\\t\\\\tfor _, d := range distributions {\\\\n173: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t// don't include 0% rollouts\\\\n174: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tif d.Rollout \\\\u003e 0 {\\\\n175: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\tvalidDistributions = append(validDistributions, d)\\\\n176: \\\\n177: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\tif buckets == nil {\\\\n178: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\tbucket := int(d.Rollout * percentMultiplier)\\\\n179: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\tbuckets = append(buckets, bucket)\\\\n180: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\t} else {\\\\n181: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\tbucket := buckets[len(buckets)-1] + int(d.Rollout*percentMultiplier)\\\\n182: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\tbuckets = append(buckets, bucket)\\\\n183: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t\\\\t}\\\\n184: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t}\\\\n185: \\\\t\\\\t}\\\\n186: \\\\n187: \\\\t\\\\t// no distributions for rule\\\\n188: \\\\t\\\\tif len(validDistributions) == 0 {\\\\n189: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\te.logger.Info(\\\\\\\"no distributions for rule\\\\\\\")\\\\n190: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tresp.Match = true\\\\n191: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_MATCH_EVALUATION_REASON\\\\n192: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\treturn resp, nil\\\\n193: \\\\t\\\\t}\\\\n194: \\\\n195: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t// as specified by Search. The return value is the index to insert x if x is\\\\n199: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\t// not present (it could be len(a)).\\\\n200: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tindex = sort.SearchInts(buckets, int(bucket)+1)\\\\n201: \\\\n210: \\\\t\\\\td := validDistributions[index]\\\\n211: \\\\t\\\\te.logger.Debug(\\\\\\\"matched distribution\\\\\\\", zap.Reflect(\\\\\\\"evaluation_distribution\\\\\\\", d))\\\\n212: \\\\n213: \\\\t\\\\tresp.Match = true\\\\n214: \\\\t\\\\tresp.Value = d.VariantKey\\\\n215: \\\\t\\\\tresp.Attachment = d.VariantAttachment\\\\n216: \\\\t\\\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_MATCH_EVALUATION_REASON\\\\n217: \\\\t\\\\treturn resp, nil\\\\n218: \\\\t} // end rule loop\\\\n219: \\\\n220: \\\\treturn resp, nil\\\\n221: }\\\\n222: \\\\n223: // matchConstraints is a utility function that will return if all or any constraints have matched for a segment depending\\\\n224: // on the match type.\\\\n225: func (e *Evaluator) matchConstraints(evalCtx map[string]string, constraints []storage.EvaluationConstraint, segmentMatchType flipt.MatchType, entityId string) (bool, string, error) {\\\\n226: \\\\tconstraintMatches := 0\\\\n227: \\\\n228: \\\\tvar reason string\\\\n229: \\\\n230: \\\\tfor _, c := range constraints {\\\\n231: \\\\t\\\\tv := evalCtx[c.Property]\\\\n232: \\\\n233: \\\\t\\\\tvar (\\\\n234: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tmatch bool\\\\n235: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\terr   error\\\\n236: \\\\t\\\\t)\\\\n237: \\\\n238: \\\\t\\\\tswitch c.Type {\\\\n239: \\\\t\\\\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_STRING_COMPARISON_TYPE:\\\\n240: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tmatch = matchesString(c, v)\\\\n241: \\\\t\\\\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_NUMBER_COMPARISON_TYPE:\\\\n242: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tmatch, err = matchesNumber(c, v)\\\\n243: \\\\t\\\\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_BOOLEAN_COMPARISON_TYPE:\\\\n244: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tmatch, err = matchesBool(c, v)\\\\n245: \\\\t\\\\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_DATETIME_COMPARISON_TYPE:\\\\n246: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tmatch, err = matchesDateTime(c, v)\\\\n247: \\\\t\\\\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_ENTITY_ID_COMPARISON_TYPE:\\\\n248: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\tmatch = matchesString(c, entityId)\\\\n249: \\\\t\\\\tdefault:\\\\n250: \\\\t\\\\t\\\\treturn false, reason, errs.ErrInvalid(\\\\\\\"unknown constraint type\\\\\\\")\\\\n251: \"annotations\": null, \"meta\": null}], \"structuredContent\": {\"content\": \"100: \\trules, err := e.store.GetEvaluationRules(ctx, storage.NewResource(r.NamespaceKey, r.FlagKey))\\n101: \\tif err != nil {\\n102: \\t\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\\n103: \\t\\treturn resp, err\\n104: \\t}\\n105: \\n106: \\tif len(rules) == 0 {\\n107: \\t\\te.logger.Debug(\\\"no rules match\\\")\\n108: \\t\\treturn resp, nil\\n109: \\t}\\n110: \\n111: \\tvar lastRank int32\\n112: \\n113: \\t// rule loop\\n114: \\tfor _, rule := range rules {\\n115: \\t\\tif rule.Rank < lastRank {\\n116: \\t\\t\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\\n117: \\t\\t\\treturn resp, errs.ErrInvalidf(\\\"rule rank: %d detected out of order\\\", rule.Rank)\\n118: \\t\\t}\\n119: \\n120: \\t\\tlastRank = rule.Rank\\n121: \\n122: \\t\\tsegmentKeys := make([]string, 0, len(rule.Segments))\\n123: \\t\\tsegmentMatches := 0\\n124: \\n125: \\t\\tfor k, v := range rule.Segments {\\n126: \\t\\t\\tmatched, reason, err := e.matchConstraints(r.Context, v.Constraints, v.MatchType, r.EntityId)\\n127: \\t\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n128: \\t\\t\\t\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\\n129: \\t\\t\\t\\treturn resp, err\\n130: \\t\\t\\t}\\n131: \\n132: \\t\\t\\tif matched {\\n133: \\t\\t\\t\\te.logger.Debug(reason)\\n134: \\t\\t\\t\\tsegmentKeys = append(segmentKeys, k)\\n135: \\t\\t\\t\\tsegmentMatches++\\n136: \\t\\t\\t}\\n137: \\t\\t}\\n138: \\n139: \\t\\tswitch rule.SegmentOperator {\\n140: \\t\\tcase flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR:\\n141: \\t\\t\\tif segmentMatches < 1 {\\n142: \\t\\t\\t\\te.logger.Debug(\\\"did not match ANY segments\\\")\\n143: \\t\\t\\t\\tcontinue\\n144: \\t\\t\\t}\\n145: \\t\\tcase flipt.SegmentOperator_AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR:\\n146: \\t\\t\\tif len(rule.Segments) != segmentMatches {\\n147: \\t\\t\\t\\te.logger.Debug(\\\"did not match ALL segments\\\")\\n148: \\t\\t\\t\\tcontinue\\n149: \\t\\t\\t}\\n150: \\t\\t}\\n151: \\n152: \\t\\t// For legacy reasons of supporting SegmentKey.\\n153: \\t\\t// The old EvaluationResponse will return both the SegmentKey, and SegmentKeys.\\n154: \\t\\t// If there are multiple segmentKeys, the old EvaluationResponse will take the first value\\n155: \\t\\t// in the segmentKeys slice.\\n156: \\t\\tif len(segmentKeys) > 0 {\\n157: \\t\\t\\tresp.SegmentKey = segmentKeys[0]\\n158: \\t\\t\\tresp.SegmentKeys = segmentKeys\\n159: \\t\\t}\\n160: \\n161: \\t\\tdistributions, err := e.store.GetEvaluationDistributions(ctx, storage.NewID(rule.ID))\\n162: \\t\\tif err != nil {\\n163: \\t\\t\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\\n164: \\t\\t\\treturn resp, err\\n165: \\t\\t}\\n166: \\n167: \\t\\tvar (\\n168: \\t\\t\\tvalidDistributions []*storage.EvaluationDistribution\\n169: \\t\\t\\tbuckets            []int\\n170: \\t\\t)\\n171: \\n172: \\t\\tfor _, d := range distributions {\\n173: \\t\\t\\t// don't include 0% rollouts\\n174: \\t\\t\\tif d.Rollout > 0 {\\n175: \\t\\t\\t\\tvalidDistributions = append(validDistributions, d)\\n176: \\n177: \\t\\t\\t\\tif buckets == nil {\\n178: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tbucket := int(d.Rollout * percentMultiplier)\\n179: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tbuckets = append(buckets, bucket)\\n180: \\t\\t\\t\\t} else {\\n181: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tbucket := buckets[len(buckets)-1] + int(d.Rollout*percentMultiplier)\\n182: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tbuckets = append(buckets, bucket)\\n183: \\t\\t\\t\\t}\\n184: \\t\\t\\t}\\n185: \\t\\t}\\n186: \\n187: \\t\\t// no distributions for rule\\n188: \\t\\tif len(validDistributions) == 0 {\\n189: \\t\\t\\te.logger.Info(\\\"no distributions for rule\\\")\\n190: \\t\\t\\tresp.Match = true\\n191: \\t\\t\\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_MATCH_EVALUATION_REASON\\n192: \\t\\t\\treturn resp, nil\\n193: \\t\\t}\\n194: \\n195: \\t\\tvar (\\n196: \\t\\t\\tbucket = crc32Num(r.EntityId, r.FlagKey)\\n197: \\t\\t\\t// sort.SearchInts searches for x in a sorted slice of ints and returns the index\\n198: \\t\\t\\t// as specified by Search. The return value is the index to insert x if x is\\n199: \\t\\t\\t// not present (it could be len(a)).\\n200: \\t\\t\\tindex = sort.SearchInts(buckets, int(bucket)+1)\\n201: \\t\\t)\\n202: \\n203: \\t\\t// if index is outside of our existing buckets then it does not match any distribution\\n204: \\t\\tif index == len(validDistributions) {\\n205: \\t\\t\\tresp.Match = false\\n206: \\t\\t\\te.logger.Debug(\\\"did not match any distributions\\\")\\n207: \\t\\t\\treturn resp, nil\\n208: \\t\\t}\\n209: \\n210: \\t\\td := validDistributions[index]\\n211: {
  "thought": "Now I understand the full picture:\n\n1. `legacy_evaluator.go` has `Evaluate()` which:\n   - Loops through rules\n   - For each rule, checks segments\n   - Uses `rule.SegmentOperator` (which is `flipt.SegmentOperator`) to determine if the rule matches\n   - Returns `*flipt.EvaluationResponse` which does NOT include the segment operator\n\n2. `evaluation.go` has `variant()` which:\n   - Calls `s.evaluator.Evaluate(ctx, flag, r)` \n   - Gets back `*flipt.EvaluationResponse`\n   - Builds `*rpcevaluation.VariantEvaluationResponse`\n   - Does NOT set `SegmentMatchType`\n\nThe approach:\n- Add an `evaluate()` method to `Evaluator` that returns `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`\n- This method contains the core logic of `Evaluate()` but also returns the segment operator\n- `Evaluate()` calls `evaluate()` and discards the operator\n- `variant()` in evaluation.go needs to call `evaluate()` directly\n\nBut wait - `variant()` calls `s.evaluator.Evaluate()` where `s.evaluator` is an interface. I need to check what interface it is.\n\nLet me look at the server.go to understand the evaluator interface.\n\nActually, looking at the task context again:\n- \"Keep public `Evaluate()` signature unchanged; add unexported `evaluate()` returning `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`; `variant()` calls `evaluate()` directly\"\n\nSo the approach is:\n1. Add `evaluate()` to `Evaluator` struct (not the interface)\n2. `Evaluate()` calls `evaluate()` and discards the operator\n3. `variant()` in evaluation.go calls `s.evaluator.evaluate()` directly\n\nBut wait - `s.evaluator` is an interface. If `evaluate()` is unexported, it can't be part of the interface. So `variant()` would need to type-assert to `*Evaluator` to call `evaluate()`.\n\nActually, looking more carefully at the task context:\n- \"Approach: Keep public `Evaluate()` signature unchanged; add unexported `evaluate()` returning `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`; `variant()` calls `evaluate()` directly\"\n\nThis suggests that `variant()` calls `evaluate()` directly on the `Evaluator` struct. But `s.evaluator` is an interface...\n\nLet me check the server.go to understand the evaluator field type.\n\nActually, looking at the task context more carefully:\n- \"Test file (`legacy_evaluator_test.go`) calls `s.Evaluate()` \u2014 NOT changing this signature\"\n\nThis means the test calls `Evaluate()` on the `Evaluator` struct directly. So the `Evaluator` struct has `Evaluate()` as a public method.\n\nFor `variant()` to call `evaluate()`, it needs access to the `Evaluator` struct directly. Let me check if `s.evaluator` is a `*Evaluator` or an interface.\n\nLet me read the server.go file.\n"

  "thought": "Now I have a clear picture. The approach is:\n\n1. In `legacy_evaluator.go`:\n   - Add `evaluate()` method that contains the core logic of `Evaluate()` but also returns `flipt.SegmentOperator`\n   - Modify `Evaluate()` to call `evaluate()` and discard the operator\n\n2. In `evaluation.go`:\n   - Modify `variant()` to call `s.evaluator.evaluate()` and use the operator to set `SegmentMatchType`\n\nThe key insight: The segment operator is determined at line 139-150 in `legacy_evaluator.go`. When a rule matches, the operator is `rule.SegmentOperator`. I need to capture this and return it.\n\nThe simplest approach:\n- Add `evaluate()` to `Evaluator` that returns `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`\n- The `evaluate()` method is the same as `Evaluate()` but also tracks and returns the matched rule's `SegmentOperator`\n- `Evaluate()` calls `evaluate()` and discards the operator\n\nFor `variant()` in `evaluation.go`:\n- Call `s.evaluator.evaluate(ctx, flag, r)` instead of `s.evaluator.Evaluate(ctx, flag, r)`\n- Map the operator to the string: OR\u2192\"ANY\", AND\u2192\"ALL\", no match\u2192\"\"\n\nThe operator mapping:\n- `flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` \u2192 \"ANY\"\n- `flipt.SegmentOperator_AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` \u2192 \"ALL\"\n- No match (zero value, which is `OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR`) \u2192 \"\"\n\nWait, but the zero value of `flipt.SegmentOperator` is `OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` (0). So I need a way to distinguish \"no match\" from \"matched with OR operator\".\n\nLooking at the code:\n- When no rule matches, `Evaluate()` returns `resp` with `resp.Match = false`\n- When a rule matches, `resp.Match = true`\n\nSo I can use `resp.Match` to determine if a segment matched. If `resp.Match = false`, then `SegmentMatchType = \"\"`. If `resp.Match = true`, then use the operator.\n\nBut wait - there's a case where `resp.Match = false` but a rule was matched (line 204-208: \"did not match any distributions\"). In this case, the rule matched but the distribution didn't. Should we still set the segment match type?\n\nLooking at the task description: \"Field should be 'ALL', 'ANY', or '' (empty when no segment matched).\"\n\nSo the field should be set based on whether a segment matched, not whether the overall evaluation matched. But looking at the code flow:\n- Lines 139-150: The rule's segment operator determines if the rule matches\n- If the rule matches (segments matched), we proceed to check distributions\n- If distributions don't match, `resp.Match = false` but segments DID match\n\nI think the intent is: set `SegmentMatchType` based on the matched rule's segment operator, regardless of whether the distribution matched. But this is ambiguous.\n\nActually, looking at the task description more carefully: \"Maps from `flipt.SegmentOperator`: OR\u2192'ANY', AND\u2192'ALL'\". This suggests we should always map the operator when a rule is matched (segments matched), regardless of distribution outcome.\n\nBut the simplest approach that makes sense semantically: set `SegmentMatchType` based on the matched rule's segment operator when `resp.Match = true` (full match including distribution).\n\nActually, let me re-read: \"Field should be 'ALL', 'ANY', or '' (empty when no segment matched).\"\n\n\"No segment matched\" means no rule's segments matched. So if a rule's segments matched but the distribution didn't, we should still set the segment match type.\n\nBut this complicates things. Let me look at what the task says more carefully:\n\n\"Approach: Keep public `Evaluate()` signature unchanged; add unexported `evaluate()` returning `(*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error)`; `variant()` calls `evaluate()` directly\"\n\nSo `evaluate()` returns the operator. The operator is the one from the matched rule. If no rule matched, the operator would be the zero value.\n\nI think the simplest approach is:\n1. `evaluate()` returns the operator of the first rule that had its segments match (regardless of distribution)\n2. `variant()` uses this operator to set `SegmentMatchType`\n3. If no rule's segments matched, the operator is the zero value, and we set `SegmentMatchType = \"\"`\n\nBut the zero value of `flipt.SegmentOperator` is `OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR` (0), which maps to \"ANY\". So I need a sentinel value to indicate \"no match\".\n\nOne approach: use a pointer `*flipt.SegmentOperator` and return `nil` when no match.\n\nAnother approach: return a boolean `matched` along with the operator.\n\nActually, the simplest approach: return the operator only when `resp.Match = true`. When `resp.Match = false`, the operator is irrelevant and we set `SegmentMatchType = \"\"`.\n\nWait, but there's the case where segments matched but distribution didn't (lines 204-208). In this case, `resp.Match = false` but segments DID match. Should we set `SegmentMatchType`?\n\nI think for simplicity, let's only set `SegmentMatchType` when `resp.Match = true`. This is the most straightforward interpretation.\n\nActually, looking at the code again:\n- Lines 188-193: \"no distributions for rule\" \u2192 `resp.Match = true`, returns\n- Lines 204-208: \"did not match any distributions\" \u2192 `resp.Match = false`, returns\n\nSo when `resp.Match = true`, it means either:\n1. No distributions for rule (segments matched, no distribution needed)\n2. Distribution matched\n\nWhen `resp.Match = false`, it means either:\n1. No rules matched\n2. Distribution didn't match\n\nFor case 2 (distribution didn't match), the segments DID match. But the task says \"empty when no segment matched\", so we should set the operator even in this case.\n\nHmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just go with the simplest approach that makes the most sense:\n\n**Approach**: `evaluate()` returns the operator of the matched rule (the rule whose segments matched). If no rule's segments matched, return the zero value. In `variant()`, check if `resp.Match` is true OR if `resp.SegmentKeys` is non-empty to determine if segments matched.\n\nActually, the simplest approach: track the operator of the first rule that had its segments match (regardless of distribution outcome). Return this operator from `evaluate()`. In `variant()`, if `resp.SegmentKeys` is non-empty, use the operator; otherwise, set `SegmentMatchType = \"\"`.\n\nWait, but `resp.SegmentKeys` is only set when segments match (line 156-159). So if `resp.SegmentKeys` is non-empty, segments matched.\n\nLet me go with this approach:\n1. `evaluate()` returns the operator of the matched rule (the rule whose segments matched)\n2. In `variant()`, if `resp.SegmentKeys` is non-empty, use the operator to set `SegmentMatchType`; otherwise, set `SegmentMatchType = \"\"`\n\nThis is clean and simple. Let me implement it.\n\nFor the `evaluate()` method, I need to track the operator when segments match. Looking at the code:\n- Lines 139-150: The switch on `rule.SegmentOperator` determines if the rule matches\n- If the rule matches (doesn't `continue`), we proceed to check distributions\n- The operator is `rule.SegmentOperator`\n\nSo I need to capture `rule.SegmentOperator` when the rule's segments match (i.e., when we don't `continue`).\n\nHere's the plan for `evaluate()`:\n```go\nfunc (e *Evaluator) evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (*flipt.EvaluationResponse, flipt.SegmentOperator, error) {\n    // ... same as Evaluate() but track the matched rule's operator\n    var matchedOperator flipt.SegmentOperator\n    \n    // In the rule loop, after the switch on rule.SegmentOperator:\n    matchedOperator = rule.SegmentOperator\n    \n    // ... rest of the logic\n    \n    return resp, matchedOperator, nil\n}\n```\n\nAnd `Evaluate()` becomes:\n```go\nfunc (e *Evaluator) Evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (*flipt.EvaluationResponse, error) {\n    resp, _, err := e.evaluate(ctx, flag, r)\n    return resp, err\n}\n```\n\nWait, but `Evaluate()` has metrics and defer logic. I need to be careful about how I refactor this.\n\nActually, looking at the code more carefully:\n- `Evaluate()` has metrics tracking (lines 47-84)\n- The core logic is lines 86-221\n\nThe cleanest approach: keep `Evaluate()` as is, but add `evaluate()` that contains just the core logic (without metrics). Then `Evaluate()` calls `evaluate()` for the core logic.\n\nBut wait - the metrics in `Evaluate()` use `resp` which is set by the core logic. So I need to be careful.\n\nActually, the simplest approach: add `evaluate()` that is a copy of `Evaluate()` but also returns the operator. Then `Evaluate()` calls `evaluate()` and discards the operator.\n\nBut this duplicates code. Let me think of a better approach.\n\nActually, the cleanest approach:\n1. Rename the current `Evaluate()` to `evaluate()` and add the operator return\n2. Add a new `Evaluate()` that calls `evaluate()` and discards the operator\n\nBut the metrics are in `Evaluate()`. If I move them to `evaluate()`, then `Evaluate()` just calls `evaluate()` and discards the operator. This is clean.\n\nLet me implement this:\n\n```go\nfunc (e *Evaluator) Evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (*flipt.EvaluationResponse, error) {\n    resp, _, err := e.evaluate(ctx, flag, r)\n    return resp, err\n}\n\nfunc (e *Evaluator) evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (resp *flipt.EvaluationResponse, matchedOperator flipt.SegmentOperator, err error) {\n    // ... all the current Evaluate() logic, plus tracking matchedOperator\n}\n```\n\nThe `matchedOperator` is set when a rule's segments match (before checking distributions). This way, even if the distribution doesn't match, we still know the operator.\n\nLet me now write the modified files.\n\nFor `legacy_evaluator.go`:\n- Change `Evaluate()` to call `evaluate()` and discard the operator\n- Add `evaluate()` with the full logic plus operator tracking\n\nFor `evaluation.go`:\n- Change `variant()` to call `s.evaluator.evaluate()` and use the operator\n\nLet me write the files now. I'll create them in the workspace.\n\nThe key changes to `legacy_evaluator.go`:\n1. `Evaluate()` becomes a thin wrapper that calls `evaluate()` and discards the operator\n2. `evaluate()` contains all the current `Evaluate()` logic plus tracks `matchedOperator`\n\nThe key change to `evaluation.go`:\n1. `variant()` calls `s.evaluator.evaluate()` instead of `s.evaluator.Evaluate()`\n2. Maps the operator to `SegmentMatchType`\n\nLet me now create the files.\n"
}

Input

{
  "command": "create",
  "file_text": "package evaluation\n\nimport (\n\t\"context\"\n\t\"encoding/json\"\n\t\"fmt\"\n\t\"hash/crc32\"\n\t\"slices\"\n\t\"sort\"\n\t\"strconv\"\n\t\"strings\"\n\t\"time\"\n\n\terrs \"go.flipt.io/flipt/errors\"\n\t\"go.flipt.io/flipt/internal/server/metrics\"\n\t\"go.flipt.io/flipt/internal/storage\"\n\t\"go.flipt.io/flipt/rpc/flipt\"\n\t\"go.flipt.io/flipt/rpc/flipt/evaluation\"\n\t\"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/attribute\"\n\t\"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/metric\"\n\t\"go.uber.org/zap\"\n)\n\n// Evaluator is an evaluator for legacy flag evaluations.\ntype Evaluator struct {\n\tlogger *zap.Logger\n\tstore  Storer\n}\n\n// NewEvaluator is the constructor for an Evaluator.\nfunc NewEvaluator(logger *zap.Logger, store Storer) *Evaluator {\n\treturn &Evaluator{\n\t\tlogger: logger,\n\t\tstore:  store,\n\t}\n}\n\nfunc (e *Evaluator) Evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (*flipt.EvaluationResponse, error) {\n\tresp, _, err := e.evaluate(ctx, flag, r)\n\treturn resp, err\n}\n\n// evaluate is the internal implementation of Evaluate that also returns the\n// SegmentOperator of the matched rule (zero value when no rule matched).\nfunc (e *Evaluator) evaluate(ctx context.Context, flag *flipt.Flag, r *evaluation.EvaluationRequest) (resp *flipt.EvaluationResponse, matchedOperator flipt.SegmentOperator, err error) {\n\t// Flag should be of variant type by default. However, for maximum backwards compatibility\n\t// on this layer, we will check against the integer value of the flag type.\n\tif int(flag.Type) != 0 || flag.Type != flipt.FlagType_VARIANT_FLAG_TYPE {\n\t\tresp = &flipt.EvaluationResponse{}\n\t\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\n\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, errs.ErrInvalidf(\"flag type %s invalid\", flag.Type)\n\t}\n\n\tvar (\n\t\tstartTime     = time.Now().UTC()\n\t\tnamespaceAttr = metrics.AttributeNamespace.String(r.NamespaceKey)\n\t\tflagAttr      = metrics.AttributeFlag.String(r.FlagKey)\n\t)\n\n\tmetrics.EvaluationsTotal.Add(ctx, 1, metric.WithAttributeSet(attribute.NewSet(namespaceAttr, flagAttr)))\n\n\tdefer func() {\n\t\tif err == nil {\n\t\t\tmetrics.EvaluationResultsTotal.Add(ctx, 1,\n\t\t\t\tmetric.WithAttributeSet(\n\t\t\t\t\tattribute.NewSet(\n\t\t\t\t\t\tnamespaceAttr,\n\t\t\t\t\t\tflagAttr,\n\t\t\t\t\t\tmetrics.AttributeMatch.Bool(resp.Match),\n\t\t\t\t\t\tmetrics.AttributeSegment.String(resp.SegmentKey),\n\t\t\t\t\t\tmetrics.AttributeReason.String(resp.Reason.String()),\n\t\t\t\t\t\tmetrics.AttributeValue.String(resp.Value),\n\t\t\t\t\t\tmetrics.AttributeType.String(\"variant\"),\n\t\t\t\t\t),\n\t\t\t\t),\n\t\t\t)\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tmetrics.EvaluationErrorsTotal.Add(ctx, 1, metric.WithAttributeSet(attribute.NewSet(namespaceAttr, flagAttr)))\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tmetrics.EvaluationLatency.Record(\n\t\t\tctx,\n\t\t\tfloat64(time.Since(startTime).Nanoseconds())/1e6,\n\t\t\tmetric.WithAttributeSet(\n\t\t\t\tattribute.NewSet(\n\t\t\t\t\tnamespaceAttr,\n\t\t\t\t\tflagAttr,\n\t\t\t\t),\n\t\t\t),\n\t\t)\n\t}()\n\n\tresp = &flipt.EvaluationResponse{\n\t\tRequestId:      r.RequestId,\n\t\tEntityId:       r.EntityId,\n\t\tRequestContext: r.Context,\n\t\tFlagKey:        r.FlagKey,\n\t\tNamespaceKey:   r.NamespaceKey,\n\t}\n\n\tif !flag.Enabled {\n\t\tresp.Match = false\n\t\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_FLAG_DISABLED_EVALUATION_REASON\n\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, nil\n\t}\n\n\trules, err := e.store.GetEvaluationRules(ctx, storage.NewResource(r.NamespaceKey, r.FlagKey))\n\tif err != nil {\n\t\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\n\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, err\n\t}\n\n\tif len(rules) == 0 {\n\t\te.logger.Debug(\"no rules match\")\n\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, nil\n\t}\n\n\tvar lastRank int32\n\n\t// rule loop\n\tfor _, rule := range rules {\n\t\tif rule.Rank < lastRank {\n\t\t\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\n\t\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, errs.ErrInvalidf(\"rule rank: %d detected out of order\", rule.Rank)\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tlastRank = rule.Rank\n\n\t\tsegmentKeys := make([]string, 0, len(rule.Segments))\n\t\tsegmentMatches := 0\n\n\t\tfor k, v := range rule.Segments {\n\t\t\tmatched, reason, err := e.matchConstraints(r.Context, v.Constraints, v.MatchType, r.EntityId)\n\t\t\tif err != nil {\n\t\t\t\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\n\t\t\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, err\n\t\t\t}\n\n\t\t\tif matched {\n\t\t\t\te.logger.Debug(reason)\n\t\t\t\tsegmentKeys = append(segmentKeys, k)\n\t\t\t\tsegmentMatches++\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tswitch rule.SegmentOperator {\n\t\tcase flipt.SegmentOperator_OR_SEGMENT_OPERATOR:\n\t\t\tif segmentMatches < 1 {\n\t\t\t\te.logger.Debug(\"did not match ANY segments\")\n\t\t\t\tcontinue\n\t\t\t}\n\t\tcase flipt.SegmentOperator_AND_SEGMENT_OPERATOR:\n\t\t\tif len(rule.Segments) != segmentMatches {\n\t\t\t\te.logger.Debug(\"did not match ALL segments\")\n\t\t\t\tcontinue\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Segments matched for this rule \u2014 record the operator.\n\t\tmatchedOperator = rule.SegmentOperator\n\n\t\t// For legacy reasons of supporting SegmentKey.\n\t\t// The old EvaluationResponse will return both the SegmentKey, and SegmentKeys.\n\t\t// If there are multiple segmentKeys, the old EvaluationResponse will take the first value\n\t\t// in the segmentKeys slice.\n\t\tif len(segmentKeys) > 0 {\n\t\t\tresp.SegmentKey = segmentKeys[0]\n\t\t\tresp.SegmentKeys = segmentKeys\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tdistributions, err := e.store.GetEvaluationDistributions(ctx, storage.NewID(rule.ID))\n\t\tif err != nil {\n\t\t\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_ERROR_EVALUATION_REASON\n\t\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, err\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tvar (\n\t\t\tvalidDistributions []*storage.EvaluationDistribution\n\t\t\tbuckets            []int\n\t\t)\n\n\t\tfor _, d := range distributions {\n\t\t\t// don't include 0% rollouts\n\t\t\tif d.Rollout > 0 {\n\t\t\t\tvalidDistributions = append(validDistributions, d)\n\n\t\t\t\tif buckets == nil {\n\t\t\t\t\tbucket := int(d.Rollout * percentMultiplier)\n\t\t\t\t\tbuckets = append(buckets, bucket)\n\t\t\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t\t\tbucket := buckets[len(buckets)-1] + int(d.Rollout*percentMultiplier)\n\t\t\t\t\tbuckets = append(buckets, bucket)\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// no distributions for rule\n\t\tif len(validDistributions) == 0 {\n\t\t\te.logger.Info(\"no distributions for rule\")\n\t\t\tresp.Match = true\n\t\t\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_MATCH_EVALUATION_REASON\n\t\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, nil\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tvar (\n\t\t\tbucket = crc32Num(r.EntityId, r.FlagKey)\n\t\t\t// sort.SearchInts searches for x in a sorted slice of ints and returns the index\n\t\t\t// as specified by Search. The return value is the index to insert x if x is\n\t\t\t// not present (it could be len(a)).\n\t\t\tindex = sort.SearchInts(buckets, int(bucket)+1)\n\t\t)\n\n\t\t// if index is outside of our existing buckets then it does not match any distribution\n\t\tif index == len(validDistributions) {\n\t\t\tresp.Match = false\n\t\t\te.logger.Debug(\"did not match any distributions\")\n\t\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, nil\n\t\t}\n\n\t\td := validDistributions[index]\n\t\te.logger.Debug(\"matched distribution\", zap.Reflect(\"evaluation_distribution\", d))\n\n\t\tresp.Match = true\n\t\tresp.Value = d.VariantKey\n\t\tresp.Attachment = d.VariantAttachment\n\t\tresp.Reason = flipt.EvaluationReason_MATCH_EVALUATION_REASON\n\t\treturn resp, matchedOperator, nil\n\t} // end rule loop\n\n\treturn resp, matchedOperator, nil\n}\n\n// matchConstraints is a utility function that will return if all or any constraints have matched for a segment depending\n// on the match type.\nfunc (e *Evaluator) matchConstraints(evalCtx map[string]string, constraints []storage.EvaluationConstraint, segmentMatchType flipt.MatchType, entityId string) (bool, string, error) {\n\tconstraintMatches := 0\n\n\tvar reason string\n\n\tfor _, c := range constraints {\n\t\tv := evalCtx[c.Property]\n\n\t\tvar (\n\t\t\tmatch bool\n\t\t\terr   error\n\t\t)\n\n\t\tswitch c.Type {\n\t\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_STRING_COMPARISON_TYPE:\n\t\t\tmatch = matchesString(c, v)\n\t\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_NUMBER_COMPARISON_TYPE:\n\t\t\tmatch, err = matchesNumber(c, v)\n\t\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_BOOLEAN_COMPARISON_TYPE:\n\t\t\tmatch, err = matchesBool(c, v)\n\t\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_DATETIME_COMPARISON_TYPE:\n\t\t\tmatch, err = matchesDateTime(c, v)\n\t\tcase flipt.ComparisonType_ENTITY_ID_COMPARISON_TYPE:\n\t\t\tmatch = matchesString(c, entityId)\n\t\tdefault:\n\t\t\treturn false, reason, errs.ErrInvalid(\"unknown constraint type\")\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif err != nil {\n\t\t\te.logger.Debug(\"error matching constraint\", zap.String(\"property\", c.Property), zap.Error(err))\n\t\t\t// don't return here because we want to continue to evaluate the other constraints\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif match {\n\t\t\t// increase the matchCount\n\t\t\tconstraintMatches++\n\n\t\t\tswitch segmentMatchType {\n\t\t\tcase flipt.MatchType_ANY_MATCH_TYPE:\n\t\t\t\t// can short circuit here since we had at least one match\n\t\t\t\tbreak\n\t\t\tdefault:\n\t\t\t\t// keep looping as we need to match all constraints\n\t\t\t\tcontinue\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t// no match\n\t\t\tswitch segmentMatchType {\n\t\t\tcase flipt.MatchType_ALL_MATCH_TYPE:\n\t\t\t\t// we can short circuit because we must match all constraints\n\t\t\t\tbreak\n\t\t\tdefault:\n\t\t\t\t// keep looping to see if we match the next constraint\n\t\t\t\tcontinue\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tvar matched = true\n\n\tswitch segmentMatchType {\n\tcase flipt.MatchType_ALL_MATCH_TYPE:\n\t\tif len(constraints) != constraintMatches {\n\t\t\treason = \"did not match ALL constraints\"\n\t\t\tmatched = false\n\t\t}\n\n\tcase flipt.MatchType_ANY_MATCH_TYPE:\n\t\tif len(constraints) > 0 && constraintMatches == 0 {\n\t\t\treason = \"did not match ANY constraints\"\n\t\t\tmatched = false\n\t\t}\n\tdefault:\n\t\treason = fmt.Sprintf(\"unknown match type %d\", segmentMatchType)\n\t\tmatched = false\n\t}\n\n\treturn matched, reason, nil\n}\n\nfunc crc32Num(entityID string, salt string) uint {\n\treturn uint(crc32.ChecksumIEEE([]byte(salt+entityID))) % totalBucketNum\n}\n\nconst (\n\t// totalBucketNum represents how many buckets we can use to determine the consistent hashing\n\t// distribution and rollout\n\ttotalBucketNum uint = 1000\n\n\t// percentMultiplier implies that the multiplier between percentage (100) and totalBucketNum\n\tpercentMultiplier float32 = float32(totalBucketNum) / 100\n)\n\nfunc matchesString(c storage.EvaluationConstraint, v string) bool {\n\tswitch c.Operator {\n\tcase flipt.OpEmpty:\n\t\treturn len(strings.TrimSpace(v)) == 0\n\tcase flipt.OpNotEmpty:\n\t\treturn len(strings.TrimSpace(v)) != 0\n\t}\n\n\tif v == \"\" {\n\t\treturn false\n\t}\n\n\tvalue := c.Value\n\n\tswitch c.Operator {\n\tcase flipt.OpEQ:\n\t\treturn value == v\n\tcase flipt.OpNEQ:\n\t\treturn value != v\n\tcase flipt.OpPrefix:\n\t\treturn strings.HasPrefix(strings.TrimSpace(v), value)\n\tcase flipt.OpSuffix:\n\t\treturn strings.HasSuffix(strings.TrimSpace(v), value)\n\tcase flipt.OpIsOneOf:\n\t\tvalues := []string{}\n\t\tif err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(value), &values); err != nil {\n\t\t\treturn false\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn slices.Contains(values, v)\n\tcase flipt.OpIsNotOneOf:\n\t\tvalues := []string{}\n\t\tif err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(value), &values); err != nil {\n\t\t\treturn false\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn !slices.Contains(values, v)\n\t}\n\n\treturn false\n}\n\nfunc matchesNumber(c storage.EvaluationConstraint, v string) (bool, error) {\n\tswitch c.Operator {\n\tcase flipt.OpNotPresent:\n\t\treturn len(strings.TrimSpace(v)) == 0, nil\n\tcase flipt.OpPresent:\n\t\treturn len(strings.TrimSpace(v)) != 0, nil\n\t}\n\n\t// can't parse an empty string\n\tif v == \"\" {\n\t\treturn false, nil\n\t}\n\n\tn, err := strconv.ParseFloat(v, 64)\n\tif err != nil {\n\t\treturn false, errs.ErrInvalidf(\"parsing number from %q\", v)\n\t}\n\n\tif c.Operator == flipt.OpIsOneOf {\n\t\tvalues := []float64{}\n\t\tif err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(c.Value), &values); err != nil {\n\t\t\treturn false, errs.ErrInvalidf(\"Invalid value for constraint %q\", c.Value)\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn slices.Contains(values, n), nil\n\t} else if c.Operator == flipt.OpIsNotOneOf {\n\t\tvalues := []float64{}\n\t\tif err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(c.Value), &values); err != nil {\n\t\t\treturn false, errs.ErrInvalidf(\"Invalid value for constraint %q\", c.Value)\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn !slices.Contains(values, n), nil\n\t}\n\n\t// TODO: we should consider parsing this at creation time since it doesn't change and it doesnt make sense to allow invalid constraint values\n\tvalue, err := strconv.ParseFloat(c.Value, 64)\n\tif err != nil {\n\t\treturn false, errs.ErrInvalidf(\"parsing number from %q\", c.Value)\n\t}\n\n\tswitch c.Operator {\n\tcase flipt.OpEQ:\n\t\treturn value == n, nil\n\tcase flipt.OpNEQ:\n\t\treturn value != n, nil\n",
  "path": "/workspace/internal/server/evaluation/legacy_evaluator.go",
  "security_risk": "MEDIUM"
}
